KY 1263 (BIG EDDY ROAD) IS CLOSED AT MILE POINT 3. THE EMBANKMENT HAS SLID AWAY INTO THE KENTUCKY RIVER AND A PORTION OF THE PAVEMENT IS GONE. THIS ROADWAY WILL BE CLOSED FOR AN EXTENDED PERIOD OF TIME. THIS AREA IS THE SAME SECTION OF KY 1263 THAT WAS CLOSED IN THE SPRING OF 2011 DUE TO A SLIDE.

AN EF-0 TORNADO TOUCHED DOWN 5 MILES SOUTH OF RICHMOND ALONG JESSICA CIRCLE. IT CAUSED TREE DAMAGE, DAMAGE TO TWO OUTBUILDINGS, AND ROOF DAMAGE TO A RESIDENCE AS IT CONTINUED ALONG A 0.6 MILE PATH TO CHEROKEE LANE. ESTIMATED PEAK WIND SPEEDS WERE 80-85 MPH AND PATH WIDTH 35 YARDS. TORNADO DURATION 1 MINUTE.

AN EF-1 TORNADO TOUCHED DOWN JUST SOUTHEAST OF THE BLUE GRASS ARMY DEPOT AT 1235 CROOKSVILLE RD OR HIGHWAY 499. THE TORNADO SNAPPED AND UPROOTED TREES ALONG ITS 2 MILE PATH SOUTHEAST OF THE BLUE GRASS ARMY DEPOT TO WALTER LAKES ROAD. IT ALSO DID MINOR PORCH AND SHINGLE DAMAGE TO A RESIDENCE. PEAK ESTIMATED WIND SPEEDS WERE 90-95 MPH AND PATH WIDTH 215 YARDS. TORNADO DURATION 3 MINUTES.

DELAYED REPORT. NWS SURVEY TEAM FOUND STRAIGHT LINE WIND DAMAGE. LARGE LIMBS BROKEN. METAL ROOF FROM BARN BLOWN OFF.

AN AERIAL SURVEY AND RADAR ANALYSIS CONCLUDED THAT AN ADDITIONAL POCKET OF STRAIGHT LINE WIND DAMAGE OCCURRED 1.5 MILES ENE OF ROUNDHILL AND ABOUT 3 MILES WSW OF THE PREVIOUSLY SURVEYED EF-2 TORNADO. AERIAL PHOTOS CONFIRM ABOUT 20 TO 30 HARDWOOD TREES MOSTLY UPROOTED AND LAYING IN A DIVERGENT PATTERN. ESTIMATED WINDS WERE 70 MPH. THE SURVEY ALSO CONFIRMED THE CORRECT START AND END POINTS OF THE EF-2 TORNADO.
PRIVATE WEATHER STATION IN HUFF, KY.
NWS STORM SURVEY FOUND EF-2 TORNADO DAMAGE APPROXIMATELY 6 MILES NW OF BROWNSVILLE. ESTIMATED PEAK WINDS OF 111-115 MPH. PATH LENGTH 0.2 MILES WITH A MAXIMUM WIDTH OF 220 YARDS.
15 TO 20 TREES DOWN.

SEVERAL TREES DOWNED IN MAMMOTH CAVE NATIONAL PARK, WITH THE LARGEST ALONG HOUCHINS FERRY RD NORTH OF THE GREEN RIVER. MEASURING INSTRUMENT 5 MILES SOUTHEAST OF THIS REPORT HAD A 47 MPH GUST AT 832 PM.
ONE TREE REPORTED DOWN.

THE TORNADO FIRST TOUCHED DOWN 6 MILES SOUTHEAST OF COLUMBIA ALONG DALE MORRISON ROAD. NUMEROUS TREES WERE UPROOTED AND A 145-FOOT BARN WAS DESTROYED, WITH THE TOP SHEARED OFF AN ADJACENT SILO. IT THEN MOVED EAST INTO A WOODED AREA, SNAPPING AND UPROOTING TREES. A MILE TO THE EAST, IT DAMAGED THREE BARNS AND UPROOTED AND SNAPPED MORE TREES ALONG MONTPELIER ROAD. A HOME NEAR THE INTERSECTION OF MONTPELIER RD AND OLD MONTPELIER RD SUSTAINED MINOR SIDING DAMAGE. AFTER TRAVELING ANOTHER 3/4 MILE EAST, THE TORNADO APPEARED TO RECYCLE, TAKING A TURN TO THE RIGHT FOR ITS LAST 1/2 MILE, WHERE IT DESTROYED PART OF A LARGE BARN AND SLID THE REMAINING STRUCTURE 2 FEET OFF IT'S FOUNDATION, PULLING SEVERAL 18' DEEP CONCRETE FOOTINGS OUT OF THE GROUND. SOUTHEAST OF THIS BARN IT AGAIN MADE A DISTINCT CUT INTO A LARGE STAND OF TREES BEFORE DISSIPATING ALONG RUSSELL CREEK JUST PAST A BRIDGE ON ACREE RD.
TREES...POWER LINES AND POWER POLES DOWN ON OLD MONTPELIER ROAD. ROOF DAMAGE TO SEVERAL HOMES. BARNS PUSHED OVER AND SILOS KNOCKED DOWN.
DOWNED TREE BLOCKING HWY 55

SURVEY TEAM LOCATED A SMALL TORNADO ROUGHLY 1 MILE EAST NORTHEAST OF TROY. DAMAGE WAS CONFINED TO AN AREA APPROXIMATELY A THIRD OF A MILE LONG AND 50 YARDS WIDE. THERE WAS SUBSTANTIAL SOFT AND HARDWOOD TREE DAMAGE IN AN AREA EAST OF THE INDUSTRIAL PARK, IN A FORESTED AREA. SOME TREES WERE SNAPPED AND OTHERS HAD LARGE BRANCHES DOWN IN THE TALLEST PORTIONS OF THE FOREST. A VIDEO OF THE TORNADO WAS INSTRUMENTAL IN THE SURVEY, ACROSS A VERY RUGGED AREA OF FOREAST AND PASTURE. BASED ON THE VIDEO, WE SUSPECT THE TORNADO MAY HAVE BEEN A LITTLE STRONGER, HOWEVER WE WERE UNABLE TO GET TO THAT DEEPLY FORESTED AREA.
